items.prompt_md: In the k-closest-points solution, why push `-dist` instead of `dist` onto the heap?
options: Negative numbers sort faster in Python
heapq is a min-heap, so negating the distance puts the farthest point at the root, ready to be evicted once the heap exceeds size k
Negating avoids floating point rounding errors
It has no real effect — it's just a style choice
items.answer: heapq is a min-heap, so negating the distance puts the farthest point at the root, ready to be evicted once the heap exceeds size k
items.explanation_md: You want to evict the *farthest* survivor when the heap overflows. Negating distance turns "farthest" into "smallest stored key," which is exactly what a min-heap's root gives you.

items.prompt_md: You need the k points closest to the origin out of n points, and k is much smaller than n. Which approach fits?
options: Fully sort all n points by distance, then take the first k
Maintain a size-k heap keyed by distance, evicting the farthest point when it grows past k
Use two pointers after sorting by x-coordinate only
Use a hash map keyed by distance value
items.answer: Maintain a size-k heap keyed by distance, evicting the farthest point when it grows past k
items.explanation_md: "Bounded best-k subset ranked by a computed key, k << n" is the fingerprint for a size-k heap — it avoids the wasted O(n log n) of sorting everything.

items.prompt_md: With n points and a bounded size-k heap, what is the overall time complexity of finding the k closest points?
items.explanation_md: Each of the n points does at most one O(log k) push and possibly one O(log k) pop, since the heap never grows past size k.
